headline: GRAINS FINISH FOUR-DAY SLIDE, METALS FALL SHARPLY
subheadline: Silver leads commodity selling as dollar strength and fund liquidation converge.
lead: Corn closed $4.18, down 4 cents and finishing a four-day slide that's erased 18 cents since Monday's open. The grain liquidation finally got its answer this week: weather didn't take control, fund selling did. Silver's 6.8% fall sharply led the commodity selling as dollar strength hit everything from gold to crude, but grains were already bleeding before the macro storm hit.
the_takeaway: The grain liquidation finished before weather could rescue it.
teaser: Four-day grain slide concludes as fund liquidation overwhelms weather support.

one_number.context: Corn dropped 18 cents from Monday's open through today's close, the sharpest four-session decline since March. Fund liquidation drove the move, not weather, as crop conditions remain generally favorable and export demand stays weak. The selling exhausted itself today with corn holding above the $4.15 technical floor.

yesterdays_call.summary: Cattle liquidation exhausted, both contracts bounced hard off Wednesday's lows.
yesterdays_call.note: Cattle held gains from yesterday's bounce while broader commodities fell apart.

sections.title: GRAINS FINISH FOUR-DAY SLIDE
sections.body: Corn finished $4.18, down 4 cents in the lightest volume of the week. The four-day liquidation that started Monday finally ran out of sellers, but not before erasing 18 cents from the July contract. Soybeans closed $11.22, down 7 cents, with **favorable crop conditions** removing any weather premium from the board. Export demand remains weak and the funds are done rotating out of grains into other sectors. December corn's 25-cent discount to July widened another penny, telling you the carry trade is working exactly as it should when nobody wants to own old crop.
sections.catalyst: Weather favorable for crop development, export demand remains weak
sections.bottom_line: Four-day slide exhausted, but no catalyst to reverse it yet.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: selling finally exhausted, volume lightest of the week

sections.title: METALS FALL SHARPLY ON DOLLAR STRENGTH
sections.body: Silver dropped 6.8% to $67.91, its biggest single-session fall since February, as **dollar strength** hit every precious metal on the board. Gold fell 2.6% to $4,350, giving back this week's premium from Iran-Iraq tensions over Strait of Hormuz shipping lanes, ongoing since late May, as diplomatic progress deflates the geopolitical bid. The DXY pushed above $100 for the first time in six weeks, and metals got the message. Industrial demand for silver remains strong, but when the dollar moves 70 basis points in a session, fundamentals take a back seat to fund positioning.
sections.bottom_line: Dollar strength overwhelmed industrial silver demand fundamentals.

sections.title: CATTLE HOLDS YESTERDAY'S BOUNCE
sections.body: Live cattle closed $241.62, flat on the day but holding yesterday's bounce from the $240 floor. Feeders added 10 cents to $353.73, showing the **tight ready supplies** that drove both contracts higher Thursday. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began Tuesday May 19 continues to constrain processing, keeping fed supplies tight even as feeder demand stays patient. Box beef cutout held steady, no new information from the cash trade, and the contracts marked time while the rest of the commodity complex fell apart.
sections.catalyst: Tight ready supplies from ongoing plant lockout
sections.bottom_line: Processing constraints still supporting both cattle contracts.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: held yesterday's bounce while broader commodities fell sharply

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm Detected in South Texas
outside_the_pit.body: Livestock vets urge calm after confirmation of the parasitic fly 31 miles from the US border. The screwworm eats warm-blooded animals alive and was eradicated from the US decades ago, but Mexico cases are climbing.

outside_the_pit.title: EU Suspends Fertilizer Tariffs for One Year
outside_the_pit.body: European Commission drops import tariffs to help farmers cut input costs, saving around €60 million on nitrogen fertilizers. Move signals global input cost pressure remains elevated despite lower commodity prices.

outside_the_pit.title: SPR Borrowers Owe 40 Million Extra Barrels
outside_the_pit.body: Energy Secretary Wright says companies that borrowed crude from Strategic Petroleum Reserve during Iran conflict owe Uncle Sam 40 million more barrels than expected. The government's emergency oil refill could be surprisingly lucrative.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: Old crop wants out, new crop can wait. The carry widened another penny today to 25 cents, exactly what you'd expect when funds are liquidating front months and producers aren't ready to price December bushels. When this spread stops widening, the grain liquidation is done.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol restart
basis.body: Ethanol grind is coming back online after spring maintenance shutdowns, and **Eastern Belt basis is responding**. Producers east of the Mississippi with old-crop bushels in storage have a window the futures board alone isn't pricing. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: The grain liquidation finished first. Weather stayed favorable all week, no premium built, and fund selling exhausted itself today in light volume. The answer was clear by Wednesday, confirmed by Friday's close.

the_more_you_know.title: 18 cents in four days: when selling has no catalyst
the_more_you_know.body: Corn's 18-cent drop this week had no weather catalyst, no USDA surprise, no export shock. Just systematic fund liquidation as managed money rotated out of grains into other sectors. When selling pressure builds without news, it tends to exhaust itself quickly-exactly what happened today in the lightest volume of the week. The market's telling you the liquidation ran its course, even if it hasn't found a reason to reverse yet.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn above 85% planted confirms Belt is ahead of schedule
Cattle cash trade: above $242 live confirms processing constraint premium
Weekly export sales: corn above 800K MT suggests demand finally firming

daily_quote.text: It's tough to make predictions, especially about the future.
